skill.name: telnetshell
skill.description: Use telnet to connect to IoT device shells, run command-based enumeration, capture logs, and review device security posture in authorized test environments.
skill.summary: Connect to telnet-accessible IoT shells and run structured security enumeration.
skill.author: BrownFineSecurity

security_audit.summary: Static analysis findings are confirmed in context. The skill is a telnet shell automation package for IoT penetration testing, but it includes explicit persistence, backdoor, credential harvesting, data extraction, firmware export, and trace removal guidance. Because these instructions enable post-exploitation and evasion, this skill should be blocked from marketplace publication.

critical_findings.title: Backdoor and Persistence Instructions
critical_findings.description: The skill provides explicit steps to add SSH authorized keys, append a telnet backdoor to startup scripts, and launch shell access on a new port. This goes beyond authorized enumeration and enables persistent unauthorized access.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced sections explicitly describe persistent SSH access and a startup backdoor. The surrounding headings identify these as post-exploitation actions, not incidental examples.

critical_findings.title: Trace Removal and Log Deletion Guidance
critical_findings.description: The examples include an automated cleanup script that deletes temporary files, removes log files, and clears shell history. These are evasion steps that reduce auditability after device access.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are presented under automated cleanup and include direct log deletion plus history clearing. This is clear post-activity concealment behavior.

critical_findings.title: Credential and Sensitive Data Extraction
critical_findings.description: The skill instructs users to read password files, search for keys and credentials, and extract configuration files, shadow files, and databases from remote devices.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple files direct users to access sensitive account, key, database, and configuration data. These instructions create a realistic data theft path.

high_findings.title: Remote Command Execution Wrapper
high_findings.description: The helper spawns telnet and sends arbitrary command strings supplied through command, script, or interactive modes to a target host. This is expected for the tool, but it provides direct remote shell automation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The helper intentionally starts a telnet client and forwards user-controlled commands. Legitimate administration is possible, but this is still high-impact remote command execution capability.

high_findings.title: Network Reconnaissance and Internal Scanning
high_findings.description: The skill includes commands for enumerating listening services, routing, firewall state, and scanning a local subnet from the compromised device.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain explicit network discovery and subnet scan workflows. This is consistent with attacker-style pivot reconnaissance after shell access.

high_findings.title: Firmware and Device Data Export
high_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to dump flash partitions and transfer firmware over the network. This can expose proprietary firmware and sensitive device contents.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section is titled firmware extraction and includes device partition dump plus network transfer steps. The intent and impact are explicit.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive Session Logging Defaults
medium_findings.description: The helper logs telnet I/O to a default temporary log path. Since sessions may include commands, credentials, and sensitive output, this can create local exposure if not protected.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The logging behavior is intentional and visible. The risk depends on host permissions and session content, so the severity is medium.

medium_findings.title: Privilege Escalation Enumeration
medium_findings.description: The skill directs users to identify SUID binaries, writable service files, sudo permissions, cron jobs, and shell escape paths. These checks can support defensive audits but also enable exploitation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit privilege escalation discovery and shell escape guidance. Authorized use is possible, but they materially assist exploitation.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Search Had No Matches
low_findings.description: A targeted search for common prompt injection phrases did not find evidence of instruction override text in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The search covered obvious override and authority phrases, and no matches were found. This does not reduce the confirmed security risks elsewhere.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Telnet Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill sends arbitrary user-provided commands to remote telnet shells through single-command, batch-script, and interactive modes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The helper is designed to transmit user-supplied commands. The capability is central to the skill and confirmed by the code path.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential File Discovery
dangerous_patterns.description: The static findings for password, SSH key, and certificate discovery are true positives because the playbooks search for those exact files and patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines intentionally search for passwords, credentials, keys, and certificates. There is no benign parser context that would make these scanner hits false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cleanup Commands That Remove Evidence
dangerous_patterns.description: The example cleanup workflow removes files under temporary directories, deletes logs, and clears command history.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and comment directly indicate post-testing cleanup that removes traces. This matches the static log deletion and shell history findings.

content.value_statement: IoT telnet shells are hard to inspect consistently during authorized security tests. This skill provides a telnet helper, enumeration scripts, logging guidance, and structured workflows for device assessment.
actual_capabilities: Connects to telnet-accessible devices by host and port.
Runs single commands, interactive sessions, or command files through a Python helper.
Detects common IoT and BusyBox shell prompts.
Cleans command output by removing echoes, prompts, and ANSI codes.
Logs telnet sessions for observation and later review.
Provides prebuilt scripts for system, network, file, and security enumeration.
limitations: Requires network access to a reachable telnet service.
Requires Python 3, pexpect, and a local telnet client.
Does not verify authorization for the target device.
Includes high-risk post-exploitation examples that are not suitable for marketplace publication.

use_cases.title: Authorized IoT Lab Assessment
use_cases.description: Inspect a lab device with a known telnet shell and collect baseline system, network, and file information.

use_cases.title: BusyBox Device Triage
use_cases.description: Run repeatable commands against embedded Linux devices and normalize noisy shell output for review.

use_cases.title: Telnet Exposure Review
use_cases.description: Document reachable telnet services, authentication behavior, and device configuration during an approved audit.

prompt_templates.title: Connect to a Lab Device
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the telnet shell skill to connect to my authorized lab device at HOST on PORT and run a basic uname check with session logging.

prompt_templates.title: Run System Enumeration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the provided system enumeration script against HOST on PORT, save the session log, and summarize the device identity, kernel, storage, and BusyBox details.

prompt_templates.title: Review Network Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the authorized device network configuration through telnet and report interfaces, routes, listening services, and firewall rules.

prompt_templates.title: Assess High-Risk Commands
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the telnet shell workflow for commands that access credentials, create persistence, extract data, or remove evidence, and classify which steps should be excluded.

output_examples.input: Run a basic device inventory on an authorized telnet target.
output_examples.output: The report lists hostname, kernel version, BusyBox presence, mounted filesystems, and open services, with notes about incomplete commands.

output_examples.input: Summarize a telnet session log from an IoT camera lab test.
output_examples.output: The summary separates connection events, commands sent, command output, detected prompts, warnings, and follow-up checks.

output_examples.input: Identify unsafe examples in this skill before publication.
output_examples.output: The review flags persistence, credential extraction, firmware export, internal scanning, and cleanup guidance as unsafe marketplace content.

best_practices: Use only on devices you own or have explicit written authorization to test.
Keep session logs protected because they can contain credentials and sensitive configuration.
Limit commands to non-destructive inventory and verification tasks during routine audits.
anti_patterns: Do not use the helper against unknown public IP addresses or third-party devices.
Do not add persistence, backdoors, or SSH keys through this workflow.
Do not delete logs, clear history, or hide activity after testing.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps an AI assistant connect to telnet-accessible IoT shells and run structured command-based checks.

faq.question: Is this safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: No. The audited version includes post-exploitation, persistence, data extraction, and cleanup instructions.

faq.question: Does it require special software?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires Python 3, pexpect, and a telnet client on the local machine.

faq.question: Can it work with BusyBox shells?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper includes prompt patterns and examples for common BusyBox-based IoT devices.

faq.question: Does it store session data?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper logs telnet input and output to a local log file by default.

faq.question: What should be removed before any safe release?
faq.answer: Remove persistence, backdoor, credential harvesting, firmware export, internal scanning, and evidence cleanup guidance.
